Conversely, high intratumoral CD8+ TIL levels associate with improved survival outcomes, lower proliferation rates, and absence of vascular invasion, with patients showing negative PD-L1 expression combined with high CD8+ TILs demonstrating the most favorable prognosis. An inverse relationship exists between PD-L1 expression and CD8+ TIL density, with 73.3% of PD-L1-negative cases showing high CD8+ TIL levels while 57.6% of PD-L1-positive cases exhibited low CD8+ TIL levels. These findings establish CD8+ TIL infiltration and PD-L1 status as independent prognostic factors in cervical cancer, suggesting that the "hot" immunophenotype (PD-L1 negative, high CD8+ TILs) predicts superior clinical outcomes compared to the "cold" phenotype.

title: The relationship between atypical glandular cells and malignancy risk: a retrospective single-center study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective single-center study demonstrates that atypical glandular cells (AGC), particularly the AGC-favor neoplastic subcategory, serve as significant predictors of malignancy risk across multiple reproductive sites. AGC was strongly associated with long-term risk of cervical adenocarcinoma, with precancerous lesions and malignancies diagnosed in 58.9% of AGC cases, including endometrial carcinoma (24.4%) as the most common finding. High-risk HPV types, especially HPV16 and HPV18/45, were predominant in cervical adenocarcinoma and adenocarcinoma in situ, while endometrial and pelvic malignancies showed inverse associations with HPV positivity, occurring more frequently in hrHPV-negative patients. Age emerged as an independent risk factor, with women aged ≥50 years showing elevated endometrial and pelvic malignancy risk, whereas younger women had higher cervical lesion prevalence. The cytological diagnosis of AGC demonstrated high predictive value for identifying patients at risk for cervical, endometrial, and pelvic pathology, suggesting its utility as a clinical marker for comprehensive gynecological surveillance and follow-up.

title: Low levels of influenza H5N1 HA and NA antibodies in the human population are boosted by seasonal H1N1 infection but not by H3N2 infection or influenza vaccination
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that the human population lacks protective antibodies against avian H5N1 influenza, with the vast majority showing undetectable H5 hemagglutinin (HA) antibodies despite the high pandemic threat and 52% case fatality rate of H5N1 infections. Notably, infection with seasonal H1N1 virus—but not H3N2 infection or seasonal vaccination—significantly boosts cross-reactive antibodies against H5N1 neuraminidase (NA), because human seasonal H1N1 viruses encode an NA protein structurally similar to H5N1 NA. While seasonal influenza vaccination induces robust responses against vaccine strains, it fails to increase binding or neutralizing antibodies against H5N1 targets, underscoring the limitations of HA-focused vaccine strategies. The findings demonstrate that NA-targeting antibodies, which are reliably induced by natural infection but not vaccination, represent an important but underappreciated component of pre-existing immunity against pandemic H5N1. These results suggest that infection history and original antigenic sin—rather than vaccination status—determine population-level heterogeneity in H5N1 susceptibility and have important implications for pandemic preparedness and the design of universal influenza vaccines.

title: Influence of infertility-associated autoantibodies and sexually transmitted infections on implantation success rates after ICSI
summary: This study examined how infertility-associated autoantibodies and sexually transmitted infections affect implantation success in intracytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI) cycles. The research identified Ureaplasma urealyticum as a particularly significant pathogen, with antibodies against it occurring more frequently in women experiencing implantation failure and the infection causing chronic endometritis, a condition strongly linked to recurrent implantation failure. Multiple other sexually transmitted infections and genital pathogens including Mycoplasma species and Chlamydia trachomatis were associated with female infertility through inflammatory pathways that promote tubal scarring and occlusion. Autoimmune factors including anti-sperm and anti-ovarian antibodies were detected in the study population, with antiphospholipid antibodies showing strong correlation with implantation failure. The analysis demonstrated that preimplantation genetic testing (including PGT-A and PGT-SR) effectively reduces both implantation failure and miscarriage rates by selecting euploid embryos. Advanced maternal age and endometrial inflammation emerge as additional critical factors compromising implantation success, suggesting that comprehensive screening for infections and autoimmune markers may help identify at-risk patients requiring targeted interventions.

title: Empowering sexual self-care in HPV-positive women: A randomized trial of health belief model–Based education
summary: This randomized controlled trial evaluated a Health Belief Model–based educational intervention designed to improve sexual self-care practices in HPV-positive women. High-risk HPV infection is causally associated with multiple cancers including cervical, anal, and oropharyngeal malignancies, with risk amplified by high-risk sexual behaviors such as multiple partners. The intervention successfully enhanced key Health Belief Model constructs—perceived susceptibility, perceived benefits, cues to action, and self-efficacy—while reducing perceived barriers to preventive action. Notably, the intervention group demonstrated significant improvements across multiple sexual self-care domains, including prevention of sexually transmitted infections, gynecologic cancer prevention, and unintended pregnancy prevention. These findings support that structured health education grounded in behavioral theory can meaningfully improve sexual health practices among HPV-infected women, particularly in cultural contexts where stigma and limited HPV knowledge may otherwise impede self-protective behaviors.

title: Global, regional, and national prevalence and years lived with disability due to infertility, 1990–2021: Results from the Global Burden of Disease Study 2021
summary: This Global Burden of Disease Study 2021 provides a comprehensive epidemiological analysis of infertility prevalence and disability burden from 1990-2021, identifying both mechanistic causes and demographic patterns. In women, infertility results from multiple pathophysiological conditions including diminished ovarian reserve, polycystic ovary syndrome, blocked fallopian tubes, endometriosis, cervicitis, and uterine inflammation, with obesity and reproductive hormone imbalances representing additional significant risk factors. Men experience infertility through reproductive tract obstruction, low sperm count, and abnormal sperm function, with aging reducing both sperm quantity and quality. The study reveals that female infertility accounts for approximately 50% of global infertility cases with substantially higher burden than male infertility (attributed to ~30% of cases), with peak prevalence occurring in the 35-39 year age group and disproportionate impact in East Asia and underdeveloped regions. Beyond reproductive consequences, infertility is associated with increased risk of cardiovascular disease, diabetes mellitus, ovarian cancer, and colorectal cancer, while also negatively affecting psychological well-being and potentially leading to intimate partner violence. Regional analysis highlights that infections such as chlamydia and gonorrhea significantly contribute to infertility burden in African regions, while access to contraception and safe abortion services are critical preventive factors against secondary infertility.

title: Systematic review of the literature: estimation of the most common gynecological disorders and associated factors among Kazakhstani adolescents
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review of gynecological disorders among Kazakhstani adolescents identifies polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), uterine fibroids, endometriosis, and reproductive cancers as the most prevalent non-infectious conditions in this population. PCOS, characterized by hyperandrogenism and irregular ovulation, is strongly associated with obesity and metabolic syndrome, with Kazakhstan reporting one of the world's highest PCOS prevalence rates (417.23 cases per 100,000). Endometriosis, an estrogen-dependent inflammatory disorder affecting the uterus, ovaries, and peritoneum primarily in reproductive-age women, involves both genetic and epigenetic factors, with Asian women showing higher risk than Black women. Significant barriers to early detection and intervention include cultural stigma surrounding gynecological consultations, limited reproductive health literacy, inadequate youth-friendly clinics, and menstruation taboos that discourage help-seeking behavior. The review highlights that Kazakhstan's elevated incidence rates for cervical and ovarian cancers are partly attributable to suboptimal disease screening and low HPV vaccination uptake, though preventive interventions like HPV vaccination and cervical screening can substantially reduce cancer incidence.

title: Multilevel Transcriptomic Association Analysis Reveals Key Genes and Potential Mechanisms in Endometrial, Ovarian, and Cervical Cancers
summary: This multilevel transcriptomic association analysis identified key genes and mechanistic pathways implicated in ovarian, cervical, and endometrial cancers. The gene SPX emerged as a disease-associated gene across all three cancer types, showing paradoxical regulation patterns—upregulation in ovarian cancer but downregulation in cervical cancer—while maintaining negative correlation with disease progression. A shared comorbidity signature of 22 genes was identified between ovarian and cervical cancers, including PTGR1, BACE1, C19orf53, LGALS9B, FNDC5, and others involved in cellular processes and immune regulation. Pairwise disease comparisons revealed tissue-specific associations: PARS2 linked cervical and endometrial cancers, while MTRF1L and GNS connected ovarian and endometrial cancers. Mechanistically, SPX may regulate tumor phosphate metabolism via phosphate transporters, whereas FNDC5 likely exerts tumor-suppressive effects through the PI3K/AKT signaling pathway. These findings provide a comprehensive molecular framework for understanding shared and distinct cancer pathogenesis mechanisms in women's reproductive tissues.

title: Mpox animal models: a comprehensive evaluation of susceptible species for pathogenesis studies and drug discovery
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive review evaluates animal models for monkeypox virus (MPV) pathogenesis and therapeutic development, with cynomolgus macaques emerging as the primary susceptible species for studying human disease. Monkeypox virus causes characteristic clinical manifestations in humans including fever, headache, myalgia, lymphadenopathy, and a progressive rash evolving through macular, papular, vesicular, and pustular stages, with disease severity and transmission varying between viral clades (Clade Ia showing higher virulence, Clade IIb showing enhanced person-to-person transmission). In animal models, different inoculation routes produce distinct pathological outcomes—intradermal inoculation causes localized pustules, aerosol challenge results in respiratory disease with coughing and cutaneous lesions, and intravenous challenge induces fulminant systemic disease with lymphoid depletion and multi-organ pathology. Among therapeutic interventions evaluated, the smallpox antiviral tecovirimat proved ineffective against mpox in clinical trials despite safety, while Dryvax and Modified Vaccinia Ankara vaccines demonstrated protective efficacy in challenge studies. These animal model findings provide critical preclinical frameworks for evaluating new monkeypox therapeutics and vaccines, with the choice of challenge route determining the relevance to specific human transmission and disease presentations.

title: Prevalence, Types and Contributing Factors of Female Infertility at Orotta National Referral Maternity Hospital, Eritrea: A Cross-Sectional Study
summary: This cross-sectional study from Eritrea identified multiple risk factors and etiologies contributing to female infertility, with ovulation disorders, menstrual irregularities, and polycystic ovarian syndrome emerging as the predominant causes. Menstrual dysfunction demonstrated a particularly strong association with ovulatory dysfunction (AOR: 20.35), while abnormal body mass index and menstrual irregularity were linked to ovarian infertility, whereas a normal BMI (18.5-25) was protective. Advanced maternal age exceeding 35 years significantly increased the risk of secondary infertility (AOR: 7.99), and previous pelvic inflammatory disease was a major contributor to tubal factor infertility alongside urban residence (AOR: 4.1). Hormonal abnormalities including elevated follicle-stimulating hormone and luteinizing hormone, combined with reduced progesterone levels, were detected in substantial proportions of infertile women. These findings underscore the multifactorial nature of female infertility, encompassing ovarian, tubal, and hormonal pathways that require comprehensive clinical evaluation for effective management.

title: HPV Prevalence and Genotype Distribution Among Infertile and Fertile Women of Turkish Nationality and Association with Cytology and Vaccination Status
summary:
# Summary

This study examined human papillomavirus (HPV) prevalence and genotype distribution in Turkish infertile versus fertile women, revealing important differences in viral characteristics between the two groups. HPV DNA was detected in 13.5% of infertile women and 18.0% of fertile women, with high-risk (HR) HPV genotypes predominating in infertile women (88.9%) while low-risk (LR) genotypes appeared exclusively in fertile women, primarily in multiple infections. Specific genotypes showed distinct patterns: HPV-82 and HPV-16 were most common in infertile women, while HPV-45 and HPV-16 predominated in fertile women. The study proposes that HPV may impair fertility through multiple mechanisms including sperm DNA damage, increased anti-sperm antibodies, trophoblast infection with subsequent embryonic apoptosis and implantation failure, and chronic inflammation causing cellular damage. Despite these mechanistic findings, the study observed no statistically significant overall differences in HPV prevalence between infertile and fertile groups, and the 9-valent HPV vaccine covered only 45-56% of detected genotypes in each group.

title: HPV Vaccine: An Effective but Underutilized Prevention Tool
journal: International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health
summary:
# Summary: HPV Vaccine as an Effective Cancer Prevention Tool

Human papillomavirus (HPV) infection leads to cervical cancer and precancerous lesions, particularly high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesions (H-SIL), representing a significant women's health burden. The HPV vaccine effectively prevents cervical cancer, genital warts, and HPV-related urogenital and head-neck cancers across multiple vaccine formulations, with vaccinated women showing dramatically lower rates of H-SIL (3.2% versus 80.9% in unvaccinated women) and cervical carcinoma (0% versus 32.1% in unvaccinated women). Beyond primary prevention, HPV vaccination offers secondary benefits by reducing H-SIL recurrence rates when administered post-conization and decreasing lifetime disease risk. The article emphasizes that despite strong evidence of efficacy, the HPV vaccine remains underutilized as a prevention strategy, warranting increased vaccination coverage to substantially reduce cervical cancer incidence and improve women's reproductive health outcomes.

title: Signaling pathways and targeted interventions for precancers
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive review examines the molecular mechanisms driving precancerous lesion progression across multiple tissue types and identifies targeted intervention strategies. Multiple signaling pathways including TGF-β, p53, Wnt, phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase, and mitogen-activated protein kinase drive multistep malignant transformation, with precancers defined by epithelial dysplasia, hyperplasia, metaplasia, and stromal fibrosis accompanied by genomic instability and cytologic atypia. Established interventions include HPV vaccination, which effectively prevents cervical intraepithelial neoplasia and associated cancers caused by HPV16/18, and emerging therapies such as the VGX-3100 therapeutic vaccine targeting HPV E6/E7 proteins and anti-PD-1 immune checkpoint inhibitors that exploit elevated programmed cell death ligand 1 expression in premalignant cells. Age-related factors drive precancer risk through accumulated oncogenic mutations and diminished DNA repair capacity, while tissue-specific precancerous states like Barrett's esophagus, oral leukoplakia, and acinar-ductal metaplasia serve as identifiable prevention targets. The review emphasizes that precancers retain an intact basement membrane distinguishing them from overt cancers, and interventions targeting senescence pathways show promise in arresting precancerous lesion progression.

title: Acute pancreatitis as a complication of epstein-barr virus infection: A case report and narrative review
summary: Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) causes acute pancreatitis through both direct viral injury of pancreatic cells and immune-mediated inflammatory mechanisms, though this complication remains exceedingly rare. EBV establishes persistent infection by infecting B-lymphocytes and establishing latency in memory B-cells, with potential for reactivation in immunosuppressed individuals. Beyond pancreatitis, EBV can trigger a diverse array of serious complications including spontaneous splenic rupture, myocarditis, encephalitis, and hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis. The pathogenesis of acute pancreatitis more broadly involves premature pancreatic enzyme activation, calcium signaling disruption, mitochondrial dysfunction, endoplasmic reticulum stress, and oxidative damage, with coexisting risk factors such as hypertriglyceridemia or alcohol consumption potentially lowering the threshold for EBV-related pancreatic inflammation. Diagnosis relies on detecting elevated lipase levels, VCA IgM serology for acute EBV infection, and computed tomography imaging, while management consists of conservative supportive care including fasting, intravenous nutritional support, and analgesia. Although antiviral therapy has been speculated to reduce EBV-related complications, current evidence remains limited, and most cases resolve with conservative treatment alone.

title: Bacterial Vaginosis [BV] knowledge, attitudes and behavioural changes after BV diagnosis among women enrolled in a clinical trial in Boston, USA and Vulindlela, South Africa: a qualitative study
summary: Bacterial vaginosis is a disruption of the vaginal microbiota characterized by reduced Lactobacilli and elevated anaerobic bacteria, causing symptoms including malodour, itchiness, and vaginal discharge alongside significant psychological impacts such as shame, embarrassment, and poor self-esteem. The condition is associated with serious health risks including increased susceptibility to HIV, sexually transmitted infections, pelvic inflammatory disease, and adverse pregnancy outcomes such as preterm birth. BV diagnosis prompted substantial behavioral changes among study participants, including modifications to sexual practices, increased abstinence, and intensified vaginal hygiene practices, while antibiotic treatments such as metronidazole and clindamycin remain the standard clinical approach alongside alternative remedies like boric acid and probiotics. Notable geographic differences emerged, with Boston participants showing significantly higher BV prevalence and knowledge compared to South African participants, along with greater antibiotic and product use. The psychosocial burden of BV symptoms created measurable impacts on sexual behavior and quality of life, yet most participants reported relief upon receiving clinical diagnosis and treatment, highlighting the importance of accessible, timely BV care.

title: Multizonal intraepithelial neoplasia of the lower genital tract and anus in women: terminology for defining the disease, an introduction by the British Society for Colposcopy and Cervical Pathology (BSCCP), International Anal Neoplasia Society (IANS), European Federation for Colposcopy (EFC) and British Society for the Study of Vulval Disease (BSSVD) scientific committees
summary:
# Summary

Persistent high-risk human papillomavirus (HPV) infection is the primary driver of high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesions (HSIL) and squamous cell carcinomas across the anogenital tract. Multizonal intraepithelial neoplasia (MZIN)—defined as HPV-related squamous intraepithelial lesions occurring in two or more anogenital sites—significantly elevates cancer risk and can result from either a single HPV infection spreading across multiple zones or multiple distinct HPV types. Beyond HPV infection, immunosuppression, current smoking, and immunosuppressant medications are key risk factors for MZIN development, while immunocompromised populations including people living with HIV, solid organ transplant recipients, and those with autoimmune disease face substantially elevated anal cancer incidence. Early detection and treatment of high-grade intraepithelial neoplasia, particularly anal HSIL as demonstrated in the landmark ANCHOR study, effectively prevents progression to invasive squamous cell carcinoma. The recognition of MZIN as a distinct clinical entity requiring tailored monitoring and treatment strategies reflects an evolving understanding of multifocal HPV-related neoplasia in the lower genital tract and anus.

title: Causal relationship between diet and common female diseases: a comprehensive Mendelian randomization study
summary: This comprehensive Mendelian randomization study establishes causal relationships between dietary intake and common female reproductive diseases using large-scale genome-wide association study data. Dried fruit consumption emerged as a consistent protective factor across multiple cancers, reducing risk of breast cancer, cervical cancer, and ovarian cancer, while beef intake paradoxically increased breast cancer risk but protected against ovarian cancer. For endometrial cancer, coffee consumption and frequent alcohol intake acted as risk factors, whereas protective associations were identified for endometriosis with coffee and cheese intake, contrasting with shellfish intake which increased endometriosis risk. Uterine fibroids showed strong associations with coffee and dried fruit intake as risk factors, while ovarian cysts were protected by coffee, seafood, dark chocolate, and dried fruits but increased with omelette and whole-wheat cereal consumption. Additionally, polycystic ovary syndrome showed positive associations with fresh fruit and yogurt intake, and premature ovarian failure was associated with herbal tea consumption. The study demonstrates that nutrients modulate gene expression and interact with genetic predisposition to influence disease susceptibility, highlighting dietary modification as a potential preventative strategy for these conditions.

title: Bacterial vaginosis associated with high rates of sexually transmitted infections among South African adolescent girls and young women
summary: This study examined the relationship between bacterial vaginosis (BV) and sexually transmitted infections (STIs) in South African adolescent girls and young women, finding that 83% carried at least one STI and 44.3% had BV. BV significantly increased susceptibility to multiple STIs including human papillomavirus, Chlamydia trachomatis, Neisseria gonorrhoeae, Trichomonas vaginalis, Mycoplasma genitalium, and herpes simplex virus 1/2, with BV-positive women showing substantially higher rates of co-infections. The protective mechanism against STIs involves Lactobacillus species, particularly Lactobacillus crispatus, which maintain vaginal pH, produce hydrogen peroxide, and modulate immune responses; conversely, BV is characterized by loss of Lactobacilli and proliferation of anaerobic pathogens including Gardnerella vaginalis, Prevotella species, and Atopobium vaginae. Beyond STI transmission, BV was associated with serious reproductive complications including pelvic inflammatory disease, spontaneous abortion, preterm labor, postpartum endometriosis, and infertility. Risk factors for BV included smoking and multiple sexual partners, highlighting the importance of maintaining healthy vaginal microbiota to reduce STI acquisition and adverse reproductive outcomes in this vulnerable population.

title: Evaluation of Therapeutic Options in Cervical Intraepithelial Neoplasia: A Narrative Review and Clinical Perspective
summary: Cervical intraepithelial neoplasia (CIN) represents a precancerous condition with significant risk of progression to invasive cervical cancer if left untreated, but screening procedures including cytology, HPV testing, and colposcopy have proven highly effective at preventing disease advancement. Treatment approaches vary by CIN grade: CIN 1 has favorable prognosis with high spontaneous regression rates (70–72%) and is managed with active surveillance in compliant young women, while CIN 2/3 require excisional procedures (LEEP or conization), which achieve recurrence rates of 4–12.5% depending on resection margins and persistent HPV infection status. Management decisions are strongly influenced by patient age, fertility preservation needs, HPV genotype, and vaccination history, with CIN 2 showing heterogeneous outcomes ranging from 16–77% spontaneous regression depending on these factors. HPV vaccination and cytological screening have substantially reduced cervical cancer incidence and mortality, with the Pap test alone contributing to 58% reduction in incidence and 83% reduction in mortality since 1972. Patient compliance with surveillance protocols emerged as critical for success, as poor adherence increases progression risk, and even treated CIN 3 carries persistently elevated risk for subsequent anogenital cancers.

title: Burden and Risk Factor Patterns of Reproductive System Tumors and Non-Tumor Diseases Among Asian Women, 1990–2021: Evidence from the GBD Study
summary:
# Summary

This Global Burden of Disease study examines the epidemiology of reproductive system tumors and non-tumor diseases in Asian women from 1990–2021, revealing distinct patterns in disease burden and risk factors across the region. Breast and cervical cancers emerge as leading contributors to mortality and disability-adjusted life years, with breast cancer strongly associated with modifiable lifestyle factors including high body mass index, elevated fasting glucose, high red meat intake, low physical activity, smoking, and alcohol consumption, while cervical cancer is primarily linked to smoking and unsafe sexual practices. Non-neoplastic disorders including endometriosis, uterine fibroids, polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), and infertility impose substantial burden particularly among younger women, compromising fertility and generating psychological stress. Age-specific patterns indicate that premenstrual syndrome peaks in women aged 10–24 years, endometriosis concentrates in women aged 15–54 years, and breast cancer predominantly affects those aged 45–59 years. Geographic disparities are notable, with South Asia experiencing the highest burden of reproductive diseases while East Asia shows consistently lower levels, and while cervical cancer rates have declined across Asia, PCOS and infertility demonstrate the fastest increases regionally. These findings underscore the diverse etiology of reproductive health conditions in Asian women and highlight the potential for intervention through modifiable risk factor management.

title: Human Papillomavirus in Reproductive Health and Pregnancy: Clinical Implications, Outcomes, and a Comprehensive Review of Vaccination
summary:
# Summary

Persistent infection with high-risk human papillomavirus (hrHPV), particularly types 16 and 18, causes cervical intraepithelial neoplasia and accounts for approximately 70% of invasive cervical cancers, while low-risk HPV types cause anogenital warts. Pregnancy creates a unique immunological environment that promotes HPV persistence and viral transcriptional activity through reduced natural killer cell activity, decreased α-defensin production (particularly elevated estradiol-mediated suppression in the third trimester), and altered helper T-cell immunity, while trophoblast cells demonstrate susceptibility to multiple HPV strains. The most significant clinical concern for pregnant women is that cervical precancer treatment substantially increases the risk of preterm birth and mid-trimester pregnancy loss, with risk proportional to excision size; less invasive procedures like ablative treatments appear safer than excisional approaches, and cervical cerclage with vaginal progesterone may mitigate some risks. HPV vaccination is safe during pregnancy and substantially reduces vaccine-targeted types, while maternal HPV detection alone does not conclusively cause adverse pregnancy outcomes, and evidence for clinically relevant vertical transmission remains limited. Diagnostic approaches including Pap smears, HPV testing, colposcopy, and cervical biopsy can be safely performed during pregnancy, though treatment of preinvasive lesions is generally deferred until postpartum unless microinvasive disease is suspected.

title: Cervical cancer in the modern era: cutting-edge strategies for diagnosis and treatment
summary:
# Summary

High-risk human papillomavirus types 16, 18, and 45 are the primary causes of cervical cancer, accounting for approximately 70% of squamous cell carcinomas and 95% of adenocarcinomas, with high-grade lesions typically progressing to cancer over 20-30 years. However, HPV-independent adenocarcinomas such as gastric-type and clear cell variants are not associated with high-risk HPV infection and tend to present at more advanced stages with poorer prognoses. Modern imaging techniques have substantially improved cervical cancer diagnosis and staging, with diffusion-weighted imaging, intravoxel incoherent motion analysis, and dynamic contrast-enhanced MRI enabling better detection of parametrial invasion and tumor grading through biomarkers like elevated T1 values and mean kurtosis in squamous cell carcinomas. Emerging molecular imaging modalities including FDG PET/MRI, hypoxia PET tracers, and fibroblast activation protein inhibitor PET offer combined anatomical and molecular information while reducing radiation exposure, making them particularly valuable for younger patients and treatment follow-up. Advanced diffusion and perfusion parameters, including texture analysis of IVIM-derived metrics, show promise in predicting the Ki-67 proliferation index and other prognostic indicators, though further validation is needed to integrate these biomarkers into routine clinical practice.

title: Cost of illness of Mycoplasma genitalium in Australia: an incidence-based approach incorporating resistance-guided therapy
summary: Mycoplasma genitalium is a sexually transmitted pathogen that causes urethritis in men and cervicitis, endometritis, and pelvic inflammatory disease in women, with a 2024 meta-analysis demonstrating a 67% increased odds of PID. Rising antimicrobial resistance—particularly macrolide resistance at 56.8% in Australia and fluoroquinolone resistance at 34.5%—threatens treatment efficacy and increases management costs. Resistance-guided therapy, which uses resistance assays to direct treatment selection (doxycycline for macrolide-susceptible infections, moxifloxacin for resistant strains, and minocycline or pristinamycin as rescue therapies), achieves higher cure rates and reduces costs compared to standard non-RGT approaches. The prevalence of macrolide resistance is a key cost driver, as increasing resistance rates lead to more first-line treatment failures under conventional therapy, necessitating additional clinic visits and second-line medications. By preventing repeat visits through superior first-line cure rates, RGT reduces overall management costs despite higher initial medication expenses. Implementation of resistance-guided therapy is thus critical for improving treatment outcomes, reducing MG-related complications including pre-term birth, and containing healthcare costs in regions with high antibiotic resistance.

title: Burden of female diseases among adolescents and young adults aged 10–24 years in South Asia and Sub-Saharan Africa, 1990–2021: a systematic analysis from the Global Burden of Disease Study 2021
summary: This Global Burden of Disease study reveals that female reproductive and cancer-related diseases impose substantial health burdens on adolescents and young adults aged 10–24 years in South Asia and Sub-Saharan Africa between 1990 and 2021. Breast cancer and cervical cancer emerged as the leading malignancies causing significant mortality and disability-adjusted life-years (DALYs), with breast cancer accounting for 2.49 million DALYs and cervical cancer for 1.56 million DALYs globally. Maternal disorders, particularly maternal hemorrhage and hypertensive disorders, remain major contributors to mortality in both regions, with Sub-Saharan Africa experiencing 130 times higher maternal mortality than Europe, while abortion and miscarriage represent the leading cause of maternal morbidity. Between 1990 and 2021, all major female cancers—breast, cervical, ovarian, and uterine—showed increasing trends, with endometriosis also rising notably in Sub-Saharan Africa compared to South Asia. Gynecological diseases demonstrated rising incidence rates among females aged 15–29 years, and the disease burden disproportionately affected countries with lower socio-demographic indices, with limited diagnostic and treatment resources contributing to elevated mortality rates.

title: Distribution and trends of the global burden of female infertility attributable to sexually transmitted infections from 1990 to 2021
summary:
# Summary

Sexually transmitted infections, particularly Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae, are responsible for nearly 30% of female infertility cases globally through their progression to pelvic inflammatory disease, which damages fallopian tubes and causes tubal factor infertility. From 1990 to 2021, the global burden of STI-related infertility showed divergent trends: gonorrhea-related infertility declined worldwide while chlamydia-related secondary infertility increased slightly, with notable geographic variation where low sociodemographic index regions experienced significant burden reduction and high sociodemographic index regions exhibited rising trends. Age-stratified analysis revealed that STI-related primary infertility peaked in women aged 40–44 years and secondary infertility in those aged 40–45 years, though secondary infertility showed higher burden in younger women aged 15–34 years. Brazil demonstrated the most dramatic increases across all STI pathogens and infertility types, while Sub-Saharan African nations like Burkina Faso and Mali achieved substantial reductions in infertility burden through improved disease management. Emerging pathogens including Mycoplasma genitalium and Treponema pallidum have been identified as additional causes of pelvic inflammatory disease and infertility, expanding the scope of STI-related reproductive health consequences. This global analysis underscores STI prevention and treatment as critical interventions for reducing preventable female infertility across diverse populations.

title: Virulence attenuation of intestinal pathogenicity via combined gene deletion in duck enteritis vaccine strain restores gut microbiota balance and enhances safety
summary:
# Summary

Duck enteritis virus causes significant intestinal damage, dysbiosis, and economic losses in poultry through multiple virulence mechanisms including thymidine kinase-mediated nucleotide synthesis and glycoprotein-mediated immune evasion. A rationally attenuated vaccine strain with combined deletions of the TK, gI/gE, and gG/gJ genes (BAC-DEV-ΔTK-ΔgI/gE-ΔgG/gJ) maintained replication kinetics similar to the parent strain while eliminating clinical symptoms and pathogenic effects in vaccinated chicks. Critically, the gene-deleted vaccine strain reversed the parental vaccine's disruption of gut microbiota composition and restored microbiota diversity, whereas the conventional vaccine enriched potential pathogens and caused intestinal dysbiosis. This multi-gene deletion approach successfully attenuates viral virulence by disrupting key immune evasion mechanisms—including the gE-gI Fc receptor activity that interferes with antibody responses—while preserving vaccine efficacy and restoring healthy gut microbiota balance, establishing a safer vaccine platform with improved animal welfare outcomes.

title: Cancer Incidence in Women After Medically Assisted Reproduction
summary: This large Australian cohort study examined cancer incidence in women exposed to medically assisted reproduction (MAR), finding a mixed cancer risk profile compared to the general population. Women undergoing ART or IUI with ovarian stimulation showed elevated incidence of hormone-sensitive cancers, particularly uterine cancer (SIR 1.23-1.32) and ovarian cancer (SIR 1.18-1.23), with endometrioid subtypes predominating. Clomiphene citrate exposure was associated with notably increased uterine cancer risk (SIR 1.83), particularly in nulliparous women, and modestly elevated thyroid cancer risk. Conversely, MAR cohorts demonstrated reduced incidence of cervical cancer, lung cancer, and certain urogenital malignancies, patterns potentially explained by enhanced medical surveillance leading to earlier detection and prevention through screening. Chronic myeloid leukemia incidence was moderately increased in ART-exposed women, while invasive breast cancer incidence remained comparable to the general population. Overall, while MAR exposure was associated with modest increases in hormone-sensitive cancers, the absolute cancer burden in MAR-treated women remained comparable to the general population, suggesting that underlying infertility factors and reproductive history may contribute substantially to observed cancer associations.

title: HPV16-Positive Pelvic Nodal Squamous Cell Carcinoma with No Detectable Cervical Malignancy
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare presentation of HPV16-positive squamous cell carcinoma metastatic to the obturator lymph node with no detectable primary cervical malignancy. HPV16 was detected in nodal tissue showing diffuse p16 positivity on immunohistochemistry, while cervical evaluation revealed only a low-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion with scattered HPV16, suggesting possible regression of an occult primary tumor. The case highlights how HPV E6/E7 proteins disrupt p53 and Rb pathways to drive carcinogenesis, and explores potential immune-mediated mechanisms of tumor regression including fever-enhanced antigen presentation and CD8+ T cell activation, biopsy-induced micro-trauma, and microbiome-driven cytokine responses. The patient underwent laparoscopic radical hysterectomy with pelvic lymph node dissection followed by cisplatin-based chemoradiotherapy and remained disease-free for 56 months, demonstrating that aggressive guideline-based treatment can achieve excellent outcomes even when the primary lesion cannot be identified or has spontaneously regressed. This case underscores the importance of multidisciplinary evaluation and consideration of primary lesion regression in HPV-associated carcinomas of unknown primary origin.

title: Unveiling the prevalence of sexually transmitted infections and its etiology among married women of remote South Andaman Island in India
summary:
# Summary

This study examined the prevalence and etiology of sexually transmitted infections (STIs) among married women in remote South Andaman Island, revealing that STIs cause severe reproductive health complications including infertility, chronic pelvic pain, and increased HIV transmission risk. The research identified multiple bacterial and viral pathogens—including Chlamydia trachomatis, Neisseria gonorrhoeae, Ureaplasma parvum, Treponema pallidum, HSV-2, and HPV—with notable geographic and demographic variation; HSV-2 and T. pallidum predominated in urban areas while U. parvum was more common in rural regions. Key risk factors for STI acquisition included lack of contraceptive use, unemployment, early sexual activity, condom non-use, and inadequate STI knowledge, while married women faced particular vulnerability due to partner infidelity and limited negotiation power in sexual relationships despite being conventionally considered low-risk. Age-specific patterns emerged, with younger women more susceptible to Chlamydia and women over 35 facing higher rates of other STIs, and literacy showed protective associations against certain infections. The findings underscore that untreated STIs lead to serious sequelae such as pelvic inflammatory disease and chronic pelvic pain, emphasizing the need for improved access to prevention, education, and timely medical care in remote populations.

title: Machine learning and the role of the vaginal and fecal microbiome in miscarriage: a matched case-control study
summary: This matched case-control study used machine learning to identify microbiome-based predictors of miscarriage, revealing that dysbiotic vaginal and fecal microbiome compositions significantly increase miscarriage risk. Specific vaginal community state types associated with dysbiosis—CST-II and CST-IVB, characterized by reduced protective Lactobacillus species and increased diversity—were found to confer 6.5-fold and 4-fold increased miscarriage risks respectively. Non-vaccine type HPV infection was independently associated with nearly 4-fold increased miscarriage risk and correlated with dysbiotic microbiome patterns, likely through induction of pro-inflammatory cytokine responses. Machine learning models incorporating vaginal microbiome data alone achieved 85% accuracy in predicting miscarriage, while fecal microbiome and questionnaire data achieved 81% and 77% respectively, with combined models reaching 82% accuracy. Low vaginal Lactobacilli and elevated microbial diversity were linked to increased Th1/Th17 pro-inflammatory cytokines, suggesting an immune-mediated mechanism whereby dysbiosis compromises early pregnancy tolerance. These findings indicate that early pregnancy microbiome composition, particularly vaginal dysbiosis associated with HPV infection, represents a modifiable risk factor for euploid miscarriage.

title: Diagnostic accuracy of intraoperative frozen section at radical abdominal trachelectomy for early-stage cervical cancer
summary:
# Summary

Radical abdominal trachelectomy effectively treats early-stage cervical cancer while preserving fertility in young women with tumors smaller than 2 cm and without lymph-node metastasis. Intraoperative frozen section examination is essential for assessing oncological safety by determining adequate resection margins and detecting lymph-node metastases, though the study identified four false negatives in 361 margin assessments and one false negative among 446 lymph-node biopsies. Technical artifacts including freezing-induced tissue damage, inferior staining quality compared to permanent sections, and tissue folding account for the diagnostic discrepancies, with particular limitations when evaluating grossly enlarged lymph nodes that detected only 47.6% of metastases identified on permanent sections. Among 326 patients evaluated, 28 required intraoperative conversion to radical hysterectomy due to positive margins, lymph-node involvement, or other adverse findings identified by frozen section examination. While frozen section provides satisfactory diagnostic performance for confirming surgical eligibility criteria, clinicians must recognize its inherent limitations and complement gross inspection with systematic assessment to minimize false negatives and ensure optimal oncological outcomes.

title: Vitamin D Serum Status and Associated Factors Among Women with Cervical Lesions
summary: Vitamin D plays critical roles in immune regulation, cellular proliferation, and apoptosis through calcitriol's activation of the vitamin D receptor, making it potentially protective against cervical cancer development. The active form of vitamin D (25(OH)D3) reduces cervical cancer cell viability, promotes apoptosis, and exhibits anti-angiogenic and anti-metastatic properties in experimental systems. This study found that vitamin D deficiency and insufficiency were highly prevalent in a South African population with cervical lesions, affecting over 70% of participants, with notably elevated rates (78%) among HIV-positive women. Vitamin D status was associated with multiple risk factors including dark skin complexion, BMI, and history of genital symptoms, while HIV infection and antiretroviral therapy were linked to altered vitamin D metabolism through immune activation. Since persistent HPV infection and immune dysfunction are key drivers of cervical lesion progression, the low prevalence of vitamin D sufficiency (27%) in this at-risk population may represent a modifiable risk factor. These findings suggest that vitamin D status warrants investigation as a potential contributor to cervical pathology in regions with high HIV prevalence and limited sun exposure.

title: Effect of HPV Adult Vaccination on Serum Anti-Müllerian Hormone Levels: Paired Measurements in a Retrospective Cohort
summary:
# Article Summary

This retrospective cohort study examined whether HPV vaccination affects ovarian reserve by measuring serum anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH), a marker of ovarian function produced by granulosa cells in antral follicles. While HPV vaccination is well-established as a cornerstone of cervical cancer prevention, reducing infection with oncogenic HPV types that cause cervical and other anogenital and oropharyngeal cancers, public concerns have been raised about potential effects on ovarian function. The analysis found that vaccinated women showed smaller unadjusted AMH decline (median −0.13 ng/mL) compared to unvaccinated controls (median −0.27 ng/mL), with controls exhibiting a steeper estimated decline of −27.6% per year versus a slower decline in vaccinated women. Importantly, follow-up AMH levels showed no significant difference between groups, and this finding was consistent with large population-based epidemiologic studies and WHO assessments that have not supported increased risk of premature ovarian insufficiency or infertility following HPV vaccination. The authors conclude that HPV vaccination was not associated with clinically significant changes in ovarian reserve, providing reassurance about the safety profile of adult HPV vaccination regarding reproductive outcomes.

title: High-risk human papillomavirus and HBV, HCV, and T. pallidum association among women living with HIV in selected health facilities of Mekelle, Tigray, Northern Ethiopia
summary:
# Summary

This study documents substantial co-infection patterns among women living with HIV in northern Ethiopia, finding that 39.7% carried high-risk HPV (HR-HPV), with significant concurrent infections by hepatitis B virus (14.2%), *Treponema pallidum* (17.4%), and hepatitis C virus (2.6%). HIV-positive individuals demonstrated markedly elevated odds of HR-HPV positivity when co-infected with HBV (6.52-fold) or *T. pallidum* (3.84-fold), with HPV-16 showing the highest co-infection rates against both pathogens. Beyond cervical cancer risk, the data reveal that HPV associates with pelvic inflammatory disease, endometriosis, and tubal infertility, while HBV and HCV intensify cervical dysplasia progression through synergistic oncogenic mechanisms that damage tumor suppressor proteins. Low CD4+ counts and persistent inflammatory responses in HIV-positive women further amplify cervical cancer vulnerability through these multi-pathogen interactions. These findings underscore the critical need for integrated screening and management of multiple sexually transmitted infections in HIV-positive populations to mitigate reproductive health complications and malignancy risk.

title: Integrated genomic and proteomic profiling reveals insights into chemoradiation resistance in cervical cancer
summary:
# Integrated Genomic and Proteomic Profiling Reveals Insights into Chemoradiation Resistance in Cervical Cancer

This integrated study identified recurrent genomic alterations and dysregulated proteins underlying chemoradiation resistance in cervical cancer. Genomic profiling revealed that mutations in PIK3CA and KMT2D are prevalent in cervical cancer (27% each), while APOBEC mutagenesis represents a common mutation signature, with HPV types 16 and 18 remaining the primary causative agents. Chemoradiation-resistant tumors display distinct genomic features including EGFR amplification and STK11 deletion, along with activation of the DNA repair pathway, which contributes to treatment evasion. Proteomic analysis identified 73 dysregulated proteins in resistant cases, with STX3, SERPINB7, LBP, EMILIN2, and NQO2 notably elevated in chemoradiation-resistant tumors. STK11 and STX3 emerged as predictive biomarkers for poor chemoradiation response, while EGFR amplification presents as a promising therapeutic target for overcoming resistance. These findings provide a comprehensive molecular framework for understanding treatment resistance mechanisms and identifying patients who may benefit from alternative or targeted therapeutic approaches beyond standard cisplatin-based concurrent chemoradiotherapy.

title: Influence of human papillomavirus on semen parameters and male infertility: a single-center study
summary:
# Summary

This single-center study reveals that human papillomavirus (HPV) DNA detected in semen is significantly associated with male infertility and impaired sperm function through multiple mechanisms. HPV inhibits Aquaporin 8, a critical water transport channel in spermatozoa, leading to reduced sperm concentration and total motile sperm count in HPV-positive infertile men compared to fertile controls. While HPV-positive men paradoxically exhibit elevated kinematic parameters (increased VSL, VAP, linearity, and beat cross frequency), these alterations reflect pathological hyperactivation rather than healthy sperm function, resulting in impaired mucus penetration ability and increased morphological abnormalities including tail deformities and acrosomal defects. HPV-positive semen is associated with decreased intrauterine insemination success rates, lower blastulation rates in assisted reproduction, and increased risk of spontaneous abortion and repeated miscarriages. Multiple high-risk HPV types including HPV-52, HPV-18, HPV-31, and HPV-58 were detected in semen samples, with the virus localizing to the equatorial region of the sperm head through interaction with syndecan-1 receptors. These findings establish HPV as a potential contributory factor to idiopathic male infertility and suggest HPV screening may be warranted in couples experiencing unexplained infertility.

title: Global, Regional, and National Impact of Pelvic Inflammatory Disease: Changing Landscape from 1990 to 2021
summary:
# Summary

Pelvic inflammatory disease (PID) remains a significant global health burden, primarily caused by ascending genital tract infections from *Chlamydia trachomatis*, *Neisseria gonorrhoeae*, and emerging pathogens like *Mycoplasma genitalium*. PID leads to serious long-term consequences including infertility, ectopic pregnancies, chronic pelvic pain, and ovarian/fallopian tube cysts. The disease burden shows a strong inverse relationship with sociodemographic development, with substantially higher prevalence rates in low-income regions, particularly sub-Saharan Africa (notably Guinea-Bissau and Sierra Leone), compared to developed nations like Western Europe and the United States. Women aged 20-39 years bear the highest burden, with peak rates occurring in the 30-39 year age group, though concerning increases have been documented in women aged 35-44 years between 2010-2021. The epidemiological landscape demonstrates that strengthened sexually transmitted infection screening, antibiotic access, and sexual health education are essential interventions to address the projected continued increase in PID burden through 2050.

title: Systems Analysis of miRNA-Mediated Host Regulatory Response in HPV-Associated Cervical Malignancy
summary: This systems analysis identifies microRNA dysregulation as a critical mechanism in HPV-mediated cervical carcinogenesis. High-risk HPV types 16 and 18, which account for nearly 70% of cervical cancers worldwide, trigger malignant transformation through viral oncoproteins E6 and E7 that simultaneously disrupt multiple cellular processes including cell cycle control, DNA repair, apoptosis, and chromosomal stability. The study revealed 42 dysregulated miRNAs in HPV16-positive cells, with four miRNAs (hsa-miR-125b-5p, hsa-miR-106b-5p, hsa-miR-23b-3p, and hsa-miR-30d-5p) consistently downregulated across both HPV16 and HPV18 infections, contributing to cellular transformation and tumor progression. Network analysis identified distinct regulatory hubs for each HPV type, with SOX2 and SERPINE1 central to HPV16 malignancy, while ERBB4 and GLI1 drive HPV18-associated transformation. These findings point to CDKN2A, CD274, and GLI1 as potential therapeutic targets, offering opportunities for interventions targeting HPV-associated cancers through miRNA pathway manipulation.
